Treat this as an execution brief for Codex, Claude, OpenCode/OpenClaw, or another capable coding agent.\n\n## Product Context\n\n- Company: Republic Services (RSG)\n- Company slug: republic-services\n- Company description: Republic Services provides recycling, solid waste collection, transfer, disposal, and environmental services in the United States.\n- Product: Republic Residential Waste Collection\n- Product slug: republic-residential-waste-collection\n- Product category: Residential waste collection\n- Product homepage: https://www.republicservices.com/residents\n- Product summary: Recurring residential trash, recycling, bulk pickup, and dumpster rental services for households and local communities.\n- Why it matters: Residential collection is the public-facing edge of Republic's route-density moat and often depends on city, HOA, or neighborhood procurement decisions.\n\n## Company Thesis\n\n- Business footprint\n  Republic Services is a large U.S. environmental services company built around collection routes, transfer stations, landfills, recycling centers, and environmental solutions.\n  Its 2025 Form 10-K reported 377 collection operations, 255 transfer stations, 79 recycling centers, and a collection business that represented about 68% of total revenue.\n- Revenue mix\n  Residential collection is a visible customer-facing business, but Republic's economics also depend on small-container, large-container, transfer, landfill, environmental solutions, and recycling processing revenue.\n  The company reported $16.591 billion of 2025 revenue and $2.139 billion of net income attributable to Republic Services, showing a durable, profitable physical-infrastructure franchise.\n\n### Moat narrative\n- Republic's moat is mainly physical and contractual: route density, permitted disposal capacity, transfer stations, fleet scale, municipal and commercial contracts, regulatory compliance, and customer switching costs reinforce one another.\n- Landfill and transfer-station permitting can take years and is often opposed locally, which makes the incumbent network difficult to replicate even when local customers dislike pricing or service quality.\n\n### Decentralization narrative\n- The full business is not easy to decentralize because mixed municipal solid waste still needs safe collection, regulated transfer, permitted disposal, insurance, drivers, trucks, and reliable service continuity.\n- The strongest decentralization pressure is modular: open route optimization, cooperative procurement, transparent pickup verification, local reuse markets, composting, and smaller recycling or materials-processing loops that reduce dependence on centralized disposal.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- A realistic replacement is not one household buying a truck; it is a local hauler, municipality, cooperative, or neighborhood buying group using open routing and transparent service records to coordinate pickup more efficiently.\n- The hard parts remain permits, vehicles, labor, insurance, disposal access, and reliability, so open software is most credible when paired with local operators that already know the physical service constraints.\n\n## Existing Alternatives Worth Studying First\n\n- GraphHopper and jsprit (open-source): Open-source routing and vehicle-routing optimization components that can support local waste-route planning.. Homepage: https://www.graphhopper.com/open-source/. Repo: https://github.com/graphhopper/jsprit.\n\n## Relevant Technology Waves\n\n- Microfactories and automated mini-home production: Small, software-defined manufacturing cells could make localized production less eccentric and more default.\n\n## Useful Sources To Read Before Building\n\n- graphhopper/jsprit (GraphHopper) - https://github.com/graphhopper/jsprit\n  Why it matters: Repository for the open-source jsprit vehicle-routing solver relevant to collection-route optimization.\n- Open Source at Our Core (GraphHopper) - https://www.graphhopper.com/open-source/\n  Why it matters: Documents GraphHopper routing engine and jsprit as open-source routing and vehicle-routing projects.\n- Republic Services 2025 Form 10-K (Republic Services) - https://investor.republicservices.com/static-files/9bc2f8b9-764b-4a60-8499-033f9ac07aee\n  Why it matters: Primary source for 2025 revenue, net income, operating footprint, business mix, regulatory risks, and infrastructure moat.\n- Republic Services Market Capitalization (CompaniesMarketCap) - https://companiesmarketcap.com/republic-services/marketcap/\n  Why it matters: Market capitalization and market-rank source used for current market-cap metric.\n- Republic Services P/E Ratio (CompaniesMarketCap) - https://companiesmarketcap.com/republic-services/pe-ratio/\n  Why it matters: Trailing P/E source used for valuation metric.\n- Residential Trash & Garbage Service (Republic Services) - https://www.republicservices.com/residents\n  Why it matters: Product source for Republic's residential trash, recycling, bulk pickup, and dumpster rental services.\n\n## Required Operating Workflow\n\n1. republic-services\n- Company description: Republic Services provides recycling, solid waste collection, transfer, disposal, and environmental services in the United States.\n- Product: Republic Recycling Services\n- Product slug: republic-recycling-services\n- Product category: Recycling and materials recovery\n- Product homepage: https://www.republicservices.com/\n- Product summary: Recycling collection, processing, commodity sales, and related materials-management services.\n- Why it matters: Recycling is a smaller but strategically important part of Republic's network because it shapes diversion, customer sustainability goals, and how much material ultimately flows into centralized disposal.\n\n## Company Thesis\n\n- Business footprint\n  Republic Services is a large U.S. environmental services company built around collection routes, transfer stations, landfills, recycling centers, and environmental solutions.\n  Its 2025 Form 10-K reported 377 collection operations, 255 transfer stations, 79 recycling centers, and a collection business that represented about 68% of total revenue.\n- Revenue mix\n  Residential collection is a visible customer-facing business, but Republic's economics also depend on small-container, large-container, transfer, landfill, environmental solutions, and recycling processing revenue.\n  The company reported $16.591 billion of 2025 revenue and $2.139 billion of net income attributable to Republic Services, showing a durable, profitable physical-infrastructure franchise.\n\n### Moat narrative\n- Republic's moat is mainly physical and contractual: route density, permitted disposal capacity, transfer stations, fleet scale, municipal and commercial contracts, regulatory compliance, and customer switching costs reinforce one another.\n- Landfill and transfer-station permitting can take years and is often opposed locally, which makes the incumbent network difficult to replicate even when local customers dislike pricing or service quality.\n\n### Decentralization narrative\n- The full business is not easy to decentralize because mixed municipal solid waste still needs safe collection, regulated transfer, permitted disposal, insurance, drivers, trucks, and reliable service continuity.\n- The strongest decentralization pressure is modular: open route optimization, cooperative procurement, transparent pickup verification, local reuse markets, composting, and smaller recycling or materials-processing loops that reduce dependence on centralized disposal.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- A credible replacement path is layered: better local separation, open waste-system modeling, cooperative reuse markets, and small recycling workspaces for plastics or durable materials before they reach centralized facilities.\n- Open tools cannot replace large material recovery facilities overnight, but they can help communities decide which streams should be reused, composted, recycled locally, or still sent to industrial processors.\n\n## Existing Alternatives Worth Studying First\n\n- SwolfPy (open-source): A free, open-source municipal solid waste life-cycle assessment and optimization framework.. Homepage: https://swolfpy-project.github.io/. Repo: https://github.com/SwolfPy-Project/swolfpy.\n- Precious Plastic (open-source): An open community model for small-scale plastic recycling workspaces, machines, knowledge, and local recycling businesses.. Start by asking the user whether they already have a repository for this project.\n2. If they do not have a repository yet, ask which platform they want to use.\n3. If the user wants a new repository and a suitable CLI is installed and authenticated, offer to create it on their behalf.\n4. Prefer `gh` for GitHub when available, but support another installed platform CLI if that is what the user is using.\n5. Do not assume repository ownership, visibility, or naming. Ask first, then act.\n\n## Required Build Sequence\n\n1. Restate the target: build a free, open, decentralized, cooperative, or protocol-first alternative that creates real pressure on Republic Services's Republic Recycling Services business.\n2. Before writing implementation code, research and document the enabling protocols, interfaces, standards, and coordination mechanisms this product should rely on.\n3. Produce the high-level docs first. At minimum create:\n   - a `README.md` with the product thesis and scope;\n   - a `docs/architecture.md` describing the system shape;\n   - a `docs/protocols.md` describing APIs, protocols, data contracts, or higher-level coordination documents;\n   - a `docs/research.md` summarizing incumbent assumptions, alternatives, and what the project will deliberately not do yet.\n4. If the right answer is initially a protocol spec, design document, or interface contract instead of running code, do that first and make the repo reflect it.\n5. Only after the docs and protocols are coherent should implementation begin.\n\n## Bright Builds Requirement Adoption\n\nAdopt the Bright Builds coding and architecture requirements very early, before significant implementation work.\n\nRepository:\n- [bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements](https://github.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements)\n\nPrimary references:\n- [README](https://github.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements)\n- [AI-ADOPTION.md](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/AI-ADOPTION.md)\n\nFollow this exact decision flow:\n\n1. Confirm you are in the root of the downstream repository.\n2. Run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- status\n```\n\n3. If status reports `Repo state: installable`, run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- install --ref main\n```\n\n4. If status reports `Repo state: installed`, run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- update --ref main\n```\n\n5. If status reports `Repo state: blocked`, stop and explain the blocking files instead of forcing replacement automatically.\n6. Only use `install --force` if the user explicitly asks to replace blocked managed files.\n7. After installation or update, mention `coding-and-architecture-requirements.audit.md` as the paper trail.\n\n## Parallel Research Expectations\n\nIf your environment supports subagents, spawn bounded parallel subagents early for:\n\n- protocol and standards research;\n- incumbent and alternative product research;\n- repo/bootstrap and requirements adoption;\n- architecture and system design;\n- implementation planning and test strategy.\n\nIf your environment does not support subagents, still execute those tracks explicitly in sequence and preserve the same separation of concerns.\n\n## Implementation Expectations\n\nAfter the docs and protocols are settled:\n\n1. Choose the smallest credible first release shape: protocol/spec only, backend service, web app, full-stack app, or another justified structure.\n2. Implement the project on top of the documented contracts instead of improvising ad hoc interfaces mid-build.\n3. Prefer root-cause design over shallow feature imitation.\n4. Keep the repo production-shaped early: clear module boundaries, tests, verification commands, and documented assumptions.\n5. If the project should expose APIs, formalize request/response contracts and error behavior before broad feature work.\n\n## Required Deliverables\n\nProduce all of the following unless the user narrows scope:\n\n- repository bootstrap or repository-creation outcome;\n- Bright Builds requirements adoption outcome;\n- protocol and interface research summary;\n- high-level docs and architecture docs;\n- initial implementation plan with milestones or phases;\n- working implementation of the first meaningful slice;\n- tests and verification evidence;\n- explicit residual risks and next steps.\n\n## Done Criteria\n\nDo not declare completion until:\n\n- the docs and protocol decisions are written down;\n- the implementation matches those decisions or explains the deviation;\n- relevant tests, lint, type checks, and build steps have run, or there is a concrete reason they could not run;\n- the user has a clear path to continue shipping the project.",

Treat this as an execution brief for Codex, Claude, OpenCode/OpenClaw, or another capable coding agent.\n\n## Product Context\n\n- Company: Royal Caribbean Group (RCL)\n- Company slug: royal-caribbean-group\n- Company description: Royal Caribbean Group operates global cruise vacation brands and related travel experiences across Royal Caribbean, Celebrity Cruises, Silversea Cruises, and joint-venture brands.\n- Product: Royal Caribbean International\n- Product slug: royal-caribbean-international\n- Product category: Cruise vacation brand\n- Product homepage: https://www.royalcaribbean.com/\n- Product summary: Royal Caribbean International is Royal Caribbean Group's mass-market cruise brand focused on large ships, family vacations, entertainment, dining, private destinations, and global itineraries.\n- Why it matters: It is the group's largest cruise brand and the clearest expression of the company's scale moat: giant vessels, onboard experiences, distribution, and destination control.\n\n## Company Thesis\n\n- Cruise brand portfolio\n  Royal Caribbean Group owns and operates Royal Caribbean, Celebrity Cruises, and Silversea Cruises, and owns 50% of a joint venture operating TUI Cruises and Hapag-Lloyd Cruises.\n  Its 2024 annual report described a combined global and partner-brand fleet of 68 ships with about 166,900 berths and itineraries reaching more than 1,000 destinations on all seven continents.\n- Capital-intensive vacation platform\n  The company competes through ship scale, brand trust, onboard amenities, private destinations, distribution, loyalty, and the ability to finance new vessels.\n  Revenue recovered strongly in 2024, with total revenue of $16.5 billion and net income attributable to Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. of $2.9 billion, but the model remains exposed to ship financing, fuel, port access, safety, regulation, and discretionary travel cycles.\n\n### Moat narrative\n- Royal Caribbean Group has a strong physical and experiential moat: large modern ships, long vessel order books, global itineraries, private destinations, established travel-agent and direct-booking channels, loyalty programs, and trusted consumer brands. Replicating that system requires billions of dollars, regulatory competence, marine operations, port relationships, hospitality labor, and marketing reach.\n- The moat is not purely software-like. It depends on capital markets, shipyards, fuel economics, safety records, demand for discretionary vacations, and continued customer willingness to buy bundled floating-resort experiences.\n\n### Decentralization narrative\n- A full replacement for a large cruise line is difficult to decentralize because ocean-going passenger ships require heavy capital, certification, insurance, crew training, port coordination, and centralized safety accountability.\n- The more plausible decentralization pressure sits around the edges: open trip planning, community travel guides, cooperative shore excursions, small-vessel expedition operators, local port experiences, and transparent itinerary marketplaces that unbundle some discovery, trust, and distribution power from the cruise brand.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- A realistic replacement would not begin as a direct copy of an Icon-class ship. It would start as an open travel stack that lets travelers assemble coastal, island, ferry, rail, local guide, and small-vessel experiences with transparent pricing and portable reputation.\n- Over time, cooperative operators could coordinate itineraries, group buying, port services, and insurance around open standards, competing with the cruise bundle where local authenticity, price transparency, or lower environmental impact matter more than a floating resort.\n\n## Existing Alternatives Worth Studying First\n\n- OpenTripPlanner (open-source): OpenTripPlanner is an open-source multimodal journey-planning engine built around open data such as OpenStreetMap and GTFS.. Homepage: https://en.wikivoyage.org/.\n\n## Relevant Technology Waves\n\n- Bitcoin and Lightning as coordination rails: Proof-of-work economics, programmable payment flows, and anti-spam pricing make more digital systems capable of rewarding signal while resisting abuse.\n\n## Useful Sources To Read Before Building\n\n- OpenStreetMap Wiki - Collaboration with Wikivoyage (OpenStreetMap Wiki) - https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Wikivoyage\n  Why it matters: Source for Wikivoyage as an openly editable travel guide using OpenStreetMap data for travel maps and destination context.\n- OpenTripPlanner (OpenTripPlanner) - https://www.opentripplanner.org/\n  Why it matters: Open-source multimodal trip-planning project relevant to decentralized itinerary discovery and routing.\n- OpenTripPlanner GitHub Repository (GitHub) - https://github.com/opentripplanner/OpenTripPlanner\n  Why it matters: Repository source confirming the project's open-source implementation and development activity.\n- Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. 2024 Form 10-K (Royal Caribbean Group) - https://www.annualreports.com/HostedData/AnnualReports/PDF/NYSE_RCL_2024.pdf\n  Why it matters: Primary source for brands, fleet scale, destinations, revenue, profitability, vessel orders, and business risks.\n- Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. Treat this as an execution brief for Codex, Claude, OpenCode/OpenClaw, or another capable coding agent.\n\n## Product Context\n\n- Company: Travelers Companies (TRV)\n- Company slug: travelers-companies\n- Company description: Travelers Companies provides property and casualty insurance for businesses, organizations, and individuals.\n- Product: Business insurance\n- Product slug: travelers-business-insurance\n- Product category: Commercial property and casualty insurance\n- Product homepage: https://www.travelers.com/business-insurance\n- Product summary: Travelers offers business insurance products for companies and organizations, including commercial property, casualty, industry-specific coverage, commercial auto, workers compensation, and specialty products.\n- Why it matters: Commercial insurance is a required operating layer for many businesses, so incumbent control over underwriting, claims, distribution, and pricing can shape the cost and resilience of local commerce.\n\n## Company Thesis\n\n- Business profile\n  Travelers is a large U.S.-based property and casualty insurer with commercial, bond and specialty, and personal insurance operations. Its core products include business insurance, surety and management liability coverages, and personal auto, homeowners, and related policies.\n  The company competes through underwriting discipline, claims operations, agent and broker distribution, data, regulatory licenses, investment income, and balance-sheet capacity.\n- Financial profile\n  Travelers reported full-year 2025 net income of $6.288 billion and core income of $6.325 billion, supported by underwriting gains, higher net investment income, and favorable reserve development.\n  As of late May 2026, market-data providers placed Travelers near a $65 billion market capitalization, consistent with its inclusion in the S&P 500 top-175 expansion cohort.\n\n### Moat narrative\n- Travelers' moat is mostly institutional rather than technological: regulatory licenses, underwriting history, claims infrastructure, agency relationships, reinsurance access, capital strength, brand trust, and the ability to price risk across many lines and geographies.\n- That moat is meaningful because insurance buyers need claims-paying reliability and regulators require solvency. However, it is not immune to modular software, open insurance administration systems, parametric products, and mutual or protocol-based capital pools that can attack narrower lines of coverage.\n\n### Decentralization narrative\n- Property and casualty insurance is hard to decentralize because losses require identity, insurable interest, fraud controls, legal compliance, capital adequacy, and claims adjudication. Auto and business policies also depend on jurisdiction-specific forms and regulators.\n- The most credible decentralization pressure is therefore selective: open policy and claims infrastructure for smaller schemes, peer-governed risk pools, parametric coverage where objective data can trigger payouts, and cooperative purchasing structures that reduce dependence on a single incumbent carrier.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- A credible replacement would not clone a national insurer immediately. It would start with narrower business risks where policy terms are simple, claims can be verified with objective evidence, and local or industry groups can pool capital transparently.\n- Open policy administration, cooperative governance, and protocol-based capital pools could let trade groups, municipalities, or affinity networks buy or self-administer coverage with less dependence on proprietary carrier infrastructure.\n\n## Existing Alternatives Worth Studying First\n\n- Etherisc Generic Insurance Framework (protocol): Etherisc provides an open-source smart-contract framework for building decentralized insurance products, including policy lifecycle, risk pools, oracle integrations, and product modules.. Homepage: https://docs.etherisc.com/. Repo: https://github.com/etherisc.\n- openIMIS (open-source): openIMIS is an open-source digital public good for administering health financing and social protection programs, including beneficiary management, claims, providers, and payer workflows.. Homepage: https://www.openimis.org/. Repo: https://github.com/openimis.\n\n## Relevant Technology Waves\n\n- Bitcoin and Lightning as coordination rails: Proof-of-work economics, programmable payment flows, and anti-spam pricing make more digital systems capable of rewarding signal while resisting abuse.\n\n## Useful Sources To Read Before Building\n\n- Business and Commercial Insurance Policy Quotes (Travelers) - https://www.travelers.com/business-insurance\n  Why it matters: Primary product page for Travelers' commercial insurance offering.\n- Etherisc Documentation (Etherisc) - https://docs.etherisc.com/\n  Why it matters: Technical documentation for decentralized insurance products and the Generic Insurance Framework.\n- Etherisc Whitepaper (Etherisc) - https://docs.etherisc.com/learn/whitepaper-en\n  Why it matters: Explains Etherisc's open-source insurance framework, risk pools, protocol roles, and decentralized insurance thesis.\n- Get to Know the Software (openIMIS Initiative) - https://www.openimis.org/get-know-software\n  Why it matters: Details openIMIS features such as beneficiary, provider, payer, claims, feedback, and monitoring workflows.\n- openIMIS GitHub Organization (openIMIS Initiative) - https://github.com/openimis\n  Why it matters: Public repositories for the openIMIS software ecosystem.\n- openIMIS Home Page (openIMIS Initiative) - https://www.openimis.org/\n  Why it matters: Official overview for openIMIS as an open-source digital public good for health financing and social protection administration.\n- Personal Insurance (Travelers) - https://investor.travelers.com/newsroom/press-releases/news-details/2026/Travelers-Reports-Excellent-Fourth-Quarter-and-Full-Year-Results/default.aspx\n  Why it matters: Provides 2025 profitability, ROE, combined ratio, investment income, cash flow, and capital return data.\n\n## Required Operating Workflow\n\n1. Start by asking the user whether they already have a repository for this project.\n2. If they do not have a repository yet, ask which platform they want to use.\n3. If the user wants a new repository and a suitable CLI is installed and authenticated, offer to create it on their behalf.\n4. Prefer `gh` for GitHub when available, but support another installed platform CLI if that is what the user is using.\n5. Do not assume repository ownership, visibility, or naming. Ask first, then act.\n\n## Required Build Sequence\n\n1. Restate the target: build a free, open, decentralized, cooperative, or protocol-first alternative that creates real pressure on Travelers Companies's Business insurance business.\n2. Before writing implementation code, research and document the enabling protocols, interfaces, standards, and coordination mechanisms this product should rely on.\n3. Produce the high-level docs first. At minimum create:\n   - a `README.md` with the product thesis and scope;\n   - a `docs/architecture.md` describing the system shape;\n   - a `docs/protocols.md` describing APIs, protocols, data contracts, or higher-level coordination documents;\n   - a `docs/research.md` summarizing incumbent assumptions, alternatives, and what the project will deliberately not do yet.\n4. If the right answer is initially a protocol spec, design document, or interface contract instead of running code, do that first and make the repo reflect it.\n5. Only after the docs and protocols are coherent should implementation begin.\n\n## Bright Builds Requirement Adoption\n\nAdopt the Bright Builds coding and architecture requirements very early, before significant implementation work.\n\nRepository:\n- [bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements](https://github.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements)\n\nPrimary references:\n- [README](https://github.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements)\n- [AI-ADOPTION.md](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/AI-ADOPTION.md)\n\nFollow this exact decision flow:\n\n1. Confirm you are in the root of the downstream repository.\n2. Run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- status\n```\n\n3. If status reports `Repo state: installable`, run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- install --ref main\n```\n\n4. If status reports `Repo state: installed`, run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- update --ref main\n```\n\n5. If status reports `Repo state: blocked`, stop and explain the blocking files instead of forcing replacement automatically.\n6. Only use `install --force` if the user explicitly asks to replace blocked managed files.\n7. After installation or update, mention `coding-and-architecture-requirements.audit.md` as the paper trail.\n\n## Parallel Research Expectations\n\nIf your environment supports subagents, spawn bounded parallel subagents early for:\n\n- protocol and standards research;\n- incumbent and alternative product research;\n- repo/bootstrap and requirements adoption;\n- architecture and system design;\n- implementation planning and test strategy.\n\nIf your environment does not support subagents, still execute those tracks explicitly in sequence and preserve the same separation of concerns.\n\n## Implementation Expectations\n\nAfter the docs and protocols are settled:\n\n1. Choose the smallest credible first release shape: protocol/spec only, backend service, web app, full-stack app, or another justified structure.\n2. Implement the project on top of the documented contracts instead of improvising ad hoc interfaces mid-build.\n3. Prefer root-cause design over shallow feature imitation.\n4. Keep the repo production-shaped early: clear module boundaries, tests, verification commands, and documented assumptions.\n5. If the project should expose APIs, formalize request/response contracts and error behavior before broad feature work.\n\n## Required Deliverables\n\nProduce all of the following unless the user narrows scope:\n\n- repository bootstrap or repository-creation outcome;\n- Bright Builds requirements adoption outcome;\n- protocol and interface research summary;\n- high-level docs and architecture docs;\n- initial implementation plan with milestones or phases;\n- working implementation of the first meaningful slice;\n- tests and verification evidence;\n- explicit residual risks and next steps.\n\n## Done Criteria\n\nDo not declare completion until:\n\n- the docs and protocol decisions are written down;\n- the implementation matches those decisions or explains the deviation;\n- relevant tests, lint, type checks, and build steps have run, or there is a concrete reason they could not run;\n- the user has a clear path to continue shipping the project.",

Treat this as an execution brief for Codex, Claude, OpenCode/OpenClaw, or another capable coding agent.\n\n## Product Context\n\n- Company: Travelers Companies (TRV)\n- Company slug: travelers-companies\n- Company description: Travelers Companies provides property and casualty insurance for businesses, organizations, and individuals.\n- Product: Auto insurance\n- Product slug: travelers-auto-insurance\n- Product category: Personal auto insurance\n- Product homepage: https://www.travelers.com/personal-insurance/auto-insurance\n- Product summary: Travelers sells personal auto insurance through its personal insurance business, offering coverage for drivers alongside home and other personal lines.\n- Why it matters: Auto insurance is legally required in most U.S. markets and is one of the most common household financial products, making pricing, claims, data access, and distribution important control points.\n\n## Company Thesis\n\n- Business profile\n  Travelers is a large U.S.-based property and casualty insurer with commercial, bond and specialty, and personal insurance operations. Its core products include business insurance, surety and management liability coverages, and personal auto, homeowners, and related policies.\n  The company competes through underwriting discipline, claims operations, agent and broker distribution, data, regulatory licenses, investment income, and balance-sheet capacity.\n- Financial profile\n  Travelers reported full-year 2025 net income of $6.288 billion and core income of $6.325 billion, supported by underwriting gains, higher net investment income, and favorable reserve development.\n  As of late May 2026, market-data providers placed Travelers near a $65 billion market capitalization, consistent with its inclusion in the S&P 500 top-175 expansion cohort.\n\n### Moat narrative\n- Travelers' moat is mostly institutional rather than technological: regulatory licenses, underwriting history, claims infrastructure, agency relationships, reinsurance access, capital strength, brand trust, and the ability to price risk across many lines and geographies.\n- That moat is meaningful because insurance buyers need claims-paying reliability and regulators require solvency. However, it is not immune to modular software, open insurance administration systems, parametric products, and mutual or protocol-based capital pools that can attack narrower lines of coverage.\n\n### Decentralization narrative\n- Property and casualty insurance is hard to decentralize because losses require identity, insurable interest, fraud controls, legal compliance, capital adequacy, and claims adjudication. Auto and business policies also depend on jurisdiction-specific forms and regulators.\n- The most credible decentralization pressure is therefore selective: open policy and claims infrastructure for smaller schemes, peer-governed risk pools, parametric coverage where objective data can trigger payouts, and cooperative purchasing structures that reduce dependence on a single incumbent carrier.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- A replacement path would begin with narrow peer groups, high deductibles, supplemental covers, or parametric add-ons rather than legally mandated full auto liability coverage.\n- The strongest decentralized versions would combine telematics chosen by the driver, portable driving records, transparent pool accounting, and claims review rules that participants understand before joining.\n\n## Existing Alternatives Worth Studying First\n\n- Teambrella (hybrid): Teambrella is a peer-to-peer insurance model in which teams collectively review claims and reimburse members, with its white paper describing Bitcoin-based payment coordination.. Homepage: https://www.teambrella.com/.\n- Etherisc Generic Insurance Framework (protocol): Etherisc's open-source insurance framework can support parametric and other blockchain-based insurance products, though mainstream personal auto insurance would require additional regulated infrastructure.. Homepage: https://docs.etherisc.com/. Repo: https://github.com/etherisc.\n\n## Relevant Technology Waves\n\n- Bitcoin and Lightning as coordination rails: Proof-of-work economics, programmable payment flows, and anti-spam pricing make more digital systems capable of rewarding signal while resisting abuse.\n\n## Useful Sources To Read Before Building\n\n- Business and Commercial Insurance Policy Quotes (Travelers) - src\lib\generated\content-graph.ts:46396:        "markdown": "# Build A Free Alternative To Truist Financial's Truist One Checking\n\nYou are turning a disruptive product thesis into a real project. Treat this as an execution brief for Codex, Claude, OpenCode/OpenClaw, or another capable coding agent.\n\n## Product Context\n\n- Company: Truist Financial (TFC)\n- Company slug: truist-financial\n- Company description: Truist Financial is a U.S. financial services holding company offering consumer, small business, commercial, corporate, wealth, and insurance-related banking services.\n- Product: Truist One Checking\n- Product slug: truist-one-checking\n- Product category: Consumer checking and digital banking\n- Product homepage: https://www.truist.com/checking/truist-one-banking\n- Product summary: Truist One Checking is a consumer checking account with digital banking, debit-card access, bill pay, Zelle support, no overdraft fees, and tiered account benefits.\n- Why it matters: Checking accounts are the primary customer relationship layer for deposits, payments, identity, payroll inflows, and everyday financial habits, making them a key surface for open payment rails and federated custody experiments.\n\n## Company Thesis\n\n- Business mix\n  Truist operates primarily through Truist Bank and reports consumer and small business banking alongside wholesale banking, giving it a broad deposit, lending, payments, wealth, and advisory footprint across the U.S. Southeast, Mid-Atlantic, and national specialty businesses.\n  The company's scale comes from regulated banking relationships, branch and digital distribution, deposit funding, credit underwriting, compliance infrastructure, and cross-sold financial products rather than a single software product.\n- Registry posture\n  The most relevant Free The World comparison is not a direct replacement for a federally regulated bank charter, but the unbundling of everyday payments, custody, budgeting, and portfolio visibility into open protocols and self-hosted or federated tools.\n  Truist One Checking and Truist Wealth are useful registry anchors because they represent mass-market deposit/payments relationships and higher-touch wealth relationships where open rails can pressure fees, data lock-in, and custodial dependence.\n\n### Moat narrative\n- Truist has a durable banking moat from regulated deposit gathering, credit underwriting, customer switching friction, branch and digital reach, brand trust, and balance-sheet scale. Those advantages are hard for open-source software alone to replicate because banking products depend on licenses, risk management, capital, payments network access, and consumer protection obligations.\n- The moat is less absolute at the application layer. Personal finance interfaces, portfolio analytics, payments messaging, merchant acceptance, and some custody models can be modularized by open-source tools, federations, and protocol rails even when insured deposits and bank credit remain regulated services.\n\n### Decentralization narrative\n- Truist's core deposit and lending products are structurally centralized because they depend on a regulated bank ledger, identity controls, fraud monitoring, and centralized compliance. That makes full decentralization low for the bank itself.\n- Decentralization pressure is more credible around complementary workflows: open e-cash and Lightning-style payment layers for small payments, self-hosted portfolio dashboards for wealth visibility, and federated community custody models that reduce reliance on a single banking interface for every financial action.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- A realistic replacement path starts with separating everyday payment UX from the bank ledger: users could keep regulated bank accounts where needed while moving small payments, merchant payments, allowance flows, or community commerce to open e-cash and Lightning-compatible tools.\n- The strongest near-term pressure is not a one-click substitute for FDIC-insured checking. It is a gradual shift where wallets, federations, and merchant tools make low-value payments more portable, privacy-preserving, and less dependent on one bank's app.\n\n## Existing Alternatives Worth Studying First\n\n- Fedimint (decentralized): Fedimint is open-source federated e-cash for Bitcoin, designed so communities can operate federations and users can transact privately through e-cash, Lightning, or on-chain Bitcoin.. Homepage: https://fedimint.org/. Repo: https://github.com/fedimint/fedimint.\n- GNU Taler (protocol): GNU Taler is a free-software electronic payment system focused on privacy-preserving payments with auditable merchant income and bank integration components.. Homepage: https://taler.net/. Repo: https://git.taler.net/.\n\n## Relevant Technology Waves\n\n- Bitcoin and Lightning as coordination rails: Proof-of-work economics, programmable payment flows, and anti-spam pricing make more digital systems capable of rewarding signal while resisting abuse.\n\n## Useful Sources To Read Before Building\n\n- Fedimint architecture (Fedimint) - https://fedimint.org/docs/MiniMintDetails/Architecture\n  Why it matters: Technical architecture source for federated e-cash and trust-model discussion.\n- Fedimint: Community Custody, Private by Design (Fedimint) - https://fedimint.org/\n  Why it matters: Primary source for Fedimint's open-source federated Bitcoin e-cash model.\n- GNU Taler documentation (GNU Taler) - https://docs.taler.net/\n  Why it matters: Technical documentation source for Taler APIs and payment architecture.\n- GNU Taler documentation and resources (GNU Taler) - https://www.taler.net/en/docs.html\n  Why it matters: Documentation source for Taler components, wallet, merchant, banking, and supplemental services.\n- Truist Financial 2025 Form 10-K (Truist Financial) - https://filecache.investorroom.com/mr5ir_truist/1081/TFC_2025_Form_10-K.pdf\n  Why it matters: Annual report source for business segments, profitability, regulated banking context, and operating model.\n- Truist Financial Corporation market capitalization (CompaniesMarketCap) - https://companiesmarketcap.com/truist-financial/marketcap/\n  Why it matters: Market capitalization source used for the refreshed market-cap metric.\n- Truist Financial ratios and metrics (StockAnalysis) - https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/tfc/financials/ratios/\n  Why it matters: Public valuation-ratio source used for the approximate P/E metric.\n- Truist official website (Truist Financial) - https://www.truist.com/\n  Why it matters: Primary company website for product and business positioning.\n- Truist One Checking account (Truist Financial) - https://www.truist.com/checking/truist-one-banking\n  Why it matters: Official product page for Truist One Checking features and positioning.\n- Truist One Checking account overview and fees (Truist Financial) - https://www.truist.com/content/dam/truist-bank/us/en/documents/disclosures/personal/aog-truist-one-checking.pdf\n  Why it matters: Official checking account disclosure used for fee and account-structure context.\n\n## Required Operating Workflow\n\n1. At minimum create:\n   - a `README.md` with the product thesis and scope;\n   - a `docs/architecture.md` describing the system shape;\n   - a `docs/protocols.md` describing APIs, protocols, data contracts, or higher-level coordination documents;\n   - a `docs/research.md` summarizing incumbent assumptions, alternatives, and what the project will deliberately not do yet.\n4. If the right answer is initially a protocol spec, design document, or interface contract instead of running code, do that first and make the repo reflect it.\n5. Only after the docs and protocols are coherent should implementation begin.\n\n## Bright Builds Requirement Adoption\n\nAdopt the Bright Builds coding and architecture requirements very early, before significant implementation work.\n\nRepository:\n- [bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements](https://github.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements)\n\nPrimary references:\n- [README](https://github.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements)\n- [AI-ADOPTION.md](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/AI-ADOPTION.md)\n\nFollow this exact decision flow:\n\n1. Confirm you are in the root of the downstream repository.\n2. Run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- status\n```\n\n3. If status reports `Repo state: installable`, run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- install --ref main\n```\n\n4. If status reports `Repo state: installed`, run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- update --ref main\n```\n\n5. If status reports `Repo state: blocked`, stop and explain the blocking files instead of forcing replacement automatically.\n6. Only use `install --force` if the user explicitly asks to replace blocked managed files.\n7. After installation or update, mention `coding-and-architecture-requirements.audit.md` as the paper trail.\n\n## Parallel Research Expectations\n\nIf your environment supports subagents, spawn bounded parallel subagents early for:\n\n- protocol and standards research;\n- incumbent and alternative product research;\n- repo/bootstrap and requirements adoption;\n- architecture and system design;\n- implementation planning and test strategy.\n\nIf your environment does not support subagents, still execute those tracks explicitly in sequence and preserve the same separation of concerns.\n\n## Implementation Expectations\n\nAfter the docs and protocols are settled:\n\n1. Choose the smallest credible first release shape: protocol/spec only, backend service, web app, full-stack app, or another justified structure.\n2. Implement the project on top of the documented contracts instead of improvising ad hoc interfaces mid-build.\n3. Prefer root-cause design over shallow feature imitation.\n4. Keep the repo production-shaped early: clear module boundaries, tests, verification commands, and documented assumptions.\n5. If the project should expose APIs, formalize request/response contracts and error behavior before broad feature work.\n\n## Required Deliverables\n\nProduce all of the following unless the user narrows scope:\n\n- repository bootstrap or repository-creation outcome;\n- Bright Builds requirements adoption outcome;\n- protocol and interface research summary;\n- high-level docs and architecture docs;\n- initial implementation plan with milestones or phases;\n- working implementation of the first meaningful slice;\n- tests and verification evidence;\n- explicit residual risks and next steps.\n\n## Done Criteria\n\nDo not declare completion until:\n\n- the docs and protocol decisions are written down;\n- the implementation matches those decisions or explains the deviation;\n- relevant tests, lint, type checks, and build steps have run, or there is a concrete reason they could not run;\n- the user has a clear path to continue shipping the project.",

Treat this as an execution brief for Codex, Claude, OpenCode/OpenClaw, or another capable coding agent.\n\n## Product Context\n\n- Company: Truist Financial (TFC)\n- Company slug: truist-financial\n- Company description: Truist Financial is a U.S. financial services holding company offering consumer, small business, commercial, corporate, wealth, and insurance-related banking services.\n- Product: Truist Wealth\n- Product slug: truist-wealth\n- Product category: Wealth management and investment services\n- Product homepage: https://www.truist.com/wealth\n- Product summary: Truist Wealth provides banking, investment management, planning, digital access, and advisory services for affluent and high-net-worth clients.\n- Why it matters: Wealth management relationships combine advice, asset custody, portfolio reporting, tax-sensitive planning, lending, and trust services, creating high-value customer lock-in where open data and self-hosted analytics can reduce dependence on proprietary dashboards.\n\n## Company Thesis\n\n- Business mix\n  Truist operates primarily through Truist Bank and reports consumer and small business banking alongside wholesale banking, giving it a broad deposit, lending, payments, wealth, and advisory footprint across the U.S. Southeast, Mid-Atlantic, and national specialty businesses.\n  The company's scale comes from regulated banking relationships, branch and digital distribution, deposit funding, credit underwriting, compliance infrastructure, and cross-sold financial products rather than a single software product.\n- Registry posture\n  The most relevant Free The World comparison is not a direct replacement for a federally regulated bank charter, but the unbundling of everyday payments, custody, budgeting, and portfolio visibility into open protocols and self-hosted or federated tools.\n  Truist One Checking and Truist Wealth are useful registry anchors because they represent mass-market deposit/payments relationships and higher-touch wealth relationships where open rails can pressure fees, data lock-in, and custodial dependence.\n\n### Moat narrative\n- Truist has a durable banking moat from regulated deposit gathering, credit underwriting, customer switching friction, branch and digital reach, brand trust, and balance-sheet scale. Those advantages are hard for open-source software alone to replicate because banking products depend on licenses, risk management, capital, payments network access, and consumer protection obligations.\n- The moat is less absolute at the application layer. Personal finance interfaces, portfolio analytics, payments messaging, merchant acceptance, and some custody models can be modularized by open-source tools, federations, and protocol rails even when insured deposits and bank credit remain regulated services.\n\n### Decentralization narrative\n- Truist's core deposit and lending products are structurally centralized because they depend on a regulated bank ledger, identity controls, fraud monitoring, and centralized compliance. That makes full decentralization low for the bank itself.\n- Decentralization pressure is more credible around complementary workflows: open e-cash and Lightning-style payment layers for small payments, self-hosted portfolio dashboards for wealth visibility, and federated community custody models that reduce reliance on a single banking interface for every financial action.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- Open-source wealth tooling is most credible as a visibility and analytics layer: clients can aggregate holdings, track performance, and model allocation without giving a bank exclusive control over the interface.\n- The harder-to-replace pieces are fiduciary advice, custody, tax planning, lending, estate services, and human relationship management. Open tools can pressure reporting, transparency, and portability before they replace full-service wealth management.\n\n## Existing Alternatives Worth Studying First\n\n- Ghostfolio (open-source): Ghostfolio is open-source wealth management software for tracking stocks, ETFs, cryptocurrencies, accounts, transactions, allocation, and performance, with self-hosting support.. Homepage: https://ghostfol.io/. Repo: https://github.com/ghostfolio/ghostfolio.\n- rotki (open-source): rotki is an open-source, self-hosted portfolio manager, accounting, and analytics tool focused on privacy-preserving tracking of crypto and broader financial activity.. Homepage: https://rotki.com/. Repo: https://github.com/rotki/rotki.\n\n## Relevant Technology Waves\n\n- Bitcoin and Lightning as coordination rails: Proof-of-work economics, programmable payment flows, and anti-spam pricing make more digital systems capable of rewarding signal while resisting abuse.\n\n## Useful Sources To Read Before Building\n\n- Ghostfolio GitHub repository (Ghostfolio) - https://github.com/ghostfolio/ghostfolio\n  Why it matters: Open-source wealth management repository used as a replacement candidate for wealth dashboards.\n- Ghostfolio open startup (Ghostfolio) - https://ghostfol.io/en/open\n  Why it matters: Source for Ghostfolio's open-source positioning and transparency claims.\n- rotki open-source portfolio manager (rotki) - https://rotki.com/\n  Why it matters: Open-source, self-hosted portfolio manager source used for privacy-preserving wealth analytics comparison.\n- Truist Financial 2025 Form 10-K (Truist Financial) - https://filecache.investorroom.com/mr5ir_truist/1081/TFC_2025_Form_10-K.pdf\n  Why it matters: Annual report source for business segments, profitability, regulated banking context, and operating model.\n- Truist Financial Corporation market capitalization (CompaniesMarketCap) - https://companiesmarketcap.com/truist-financial/marketcap/\n  Why it matters: Market capitalization source used for the refreshed market-cap metric.\n- Truist Financial ratios and metrics (StockAnalysis) - https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/tfc/financials/ratios/\n  Why it matters: Public valuation-ratio source used for the approximate P/E metric.\n- Truist official website (Truist Financial) - https://www.truist.com/\n  Why it matters: Primary company website for product and business positioning.\n- Truist Wealth digital banking access (Truist Financial) - https://www.truist.com/digital-banking/digital-wealth\n  Why it matters: Official Truist Wealth digital-access page used for wealth product context.\n\n## Required Operating Workflow\n\n1. src\lib\generated\content-graph.ts:46460:        "markdown": "# Build A Free Alternative To Uber Technologies's Uber\n\nYou are turning a disruptive product thesis into a real project. Treat this as an execution brief for Codex, Claude, OpenCode/OpenClaw, or another capable coding agent.\n\n## Product Context\n\n- Company: Uber Technologies (UBER)\n- Company slug: uber-technologies\n- Company description: Uber Technologies operates global mobility, delivery, and freight marketplace platforms that connect consumers, earners, merchants, and shippers.\n- Product: Uber\n- Product slug: uber\n- Product category: Mobility marketplace\n- Product homepage: https://www.uber.com/\n- Product summary: Uber's Mobility product connects riders with drivers, taxis, rentals, public transit, micromobility, and other transportation options through a centralized marketplace.\n- Why it matters: Mobility is Uber's core demand-and-supply network, where dispatch, pricing, reputation, route matching, payments, and local compliance create a high-friction marketplace moat.\n\n## Company Thesis\n\n- Platform Scope\n  Uber reports three operating segments: Mobility, Delivery, and Freight. Mobility connects riders with transportation options, Delivery connects consumers with restaurants, grocery, convenience, alcohol, and retail merchants, and Freight provides brokerage and transportation-management services for shippers.\n  The company processed 13.6 billion trips and $193.5 billion of gross bookings in 2025, with annual revenue of $52.0 billion.\n- Current Position\n  Uber's moat is strongest where liquidity, routing, payments, trust and safety, driver supply, merchant relationships, and local regulatory operations reinforce one another. The same platform scale also creates exposure to labor classification, insurance, city-level regulation, and take-rate pressure.\n  The company was meaningfully profitable in 2025, reporting $10.1 billion of net income attributable to Uber Technologies and $8.7 billion of adjusted EBITDA.\n\n### Moat narrative\n- Uber benefits from two-sided and three-sided network effects: riders want short wait times, drivers and couriers want high utilization, and merchants want demand. Dense local supply is hard to replicate city by city, especially when the platform also bundles payments, dispatch, fraud controls, support, maps, insurance, and marketplace pricing.\n- The moat is not absolute. Mobility and delivery compete with other large platforms, taxis, restaurants' own delivery channels, grocery delivery services, autonomous-vehicle developers, and freight brokers. The strongest pressure point is local unbundling: communities can sometimes replace parts of the stack when dispatch, routing, identity, payments, and governance become cheaper and more open.\n\n### Decentralization narrative\n- Uber's core marketplace is software-mediated coordination of local physical work, which is structurally more decentralizable than a capital-heavy manufacturer but harder than a pure digital service. Drivers, couriers, merchants, fleets, and local cooperatives can own more of the relationship if they can coordinate demand, reputation, routing, insurance, and dispute resolution without relying on a single global operator.\n- The practical path is not one global open Uber clone. More credible pressure comes from federated city networks, cooperative delivery fleets, open routing and mapping infrastructure, local merchant marketplaces, and payment rails that reduce platform lock-in while preserving safety and accountability.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- A credible replacement would likely begin as city-scale or corridor-scale networks, not a universal global clone. Local driver groups, co-ops, taxi fleets, or municipalities could combine open maps, open routing, shared dispatch software, and transparent fee schedules.\n- The hard parts are trust, safety, liquidity, insurance, dispute resolution, and rider acquisition. A lightweight replacement can reduce platform rent only if it solves those local operating requirements without simply recreating a centralized gatekeeper.\n\n## Existing Alternatives Worth Studying First\n\n- LibreTaxi (open-source): LibreTaxi is a free and open-source ride-hailing project intended to connect passengers and drivers without a centralized incumbent marketplace.. Homepage: https://libretaxi.org/. Repo: https://github.com/ro31337/libretaxi.\n- Open Source Routing Machine (open-source): OSRM is an open-source routing engine for OpenStreetMap data that can provide route, table, matching, trip, and tile services for mobility applications.. Homepage: https://project-osrm.org/. Repo: https://github.com/Project-OSRM/osrm-backend.\n\n## Relevant Technology Waves\n\n- Bitcoin and Lightning as coordination rails: Proof-of-work economics, programmable payment flows, and anti-spam pricing make more digital systems capable of rewarding signal while resisting abuse.\n\n## Useful Sources To Read Before Building\n\n- LibreTaxi GitHub Repository (LibreTaxi contributors) - https://github.com/ro31337/libretaxi\n  Why it matters: Open-source ride-hailing alternative used to assess mobility replacement feasibility.\n- Project OSRM (Project OSRM) - https://project-osrm.org/\n  Why it matters: Project documentation describing OSRM's OpenStreetMap routing capabilities and license.\n- Project OSRM osrm-backend GitHub Repository (Project OSRM contributors) - https://github.com/Project-OSRM/osrm-backend\n  Why it matters: Open-source routing infrastructure relevant to decentralized mobility and delivery dispatch.\n- Uber (UBER) Market Capitalization (CompaniesMarketCap) - https://companiesmarketcap.com/uber/marketcap/\n  Why it matters: Market-cap source for the current valuation snapshot and company market-cap ranking.\n- Uber (UBER) P/E Ratio (CompaniesMarketCap) - https://companiesmarketcap.com/uber/pe-ratio/\n  Why it matters: Market-data source for Uber's trailing price-to-earnings ratio.\n- Uber IPO: Stock starts trading on the New York Stock Exchange (CNBC) - https://www.cnbc.com/2019/05/10/uber-ipo-stock-starts-trading-on-the-new-york-stock-exchange.html\n  Why it matters: Supports Uber's IPO trading date and non-diluted valuation at the IPO price.\n- Uber Technologies 2025 Annual Report (Uber Technologies, Inc.) - https://s23.q4cdn.com/407969754/files/doc_events/2026/May/04/2025-Annual-Report.pdf\n  Why it matters: Primary source for Uber's business segments, revenue, gross bookings, trips, profitability, competition, and risk context.\n\n## Required Operating Workflow\n\n1. Mobility connects riders with transportation options, Delivery connects consumers with restaurants, grocery, convenience, alcohol, and retail merchants, and Freight provides brokerage and transportation-management services for shippers.\n  The company processed 13.6 billion trips and $193.5 billion of gross bookings in 2025, with annual revenue of $52.0 billion.\n- Current Position\n  Uber's moat is strongest where liquidity, routing, payments, trust and safety, driver supply, merchant relationships, and local regulatory operations reinforce one another. More credible pressure comes from federated city networks, cooperative delivery fleets, open routing and mapping infrastructure, local merchant marketplaces, and payment rails that reduce platform lock-in while preserving safety and accountability.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- A replacement can be narrower than Uber Eats: restaurants, local shops, and couriers in one city can coordinate menus, ordering, delivery zones, and transparent fees through cooperative or open-source platforms.\n- The strongest near-term path is a hybrid of merchant-owned storefronts, cooperative courier fleets, and local food networks. Before writing implementation code, research and document the enabling protocols, interfaces, standards, and coordination mechanisms this product should rely on.\n3. Produce the high-level docs first. At minimum create:\n   - a `README.md` with the product thesis and scope;\n   - a `docs/architecture.md` describing the system shape;\n   - a `docs/protocols.md` describing APIs, protocols, data contracts, or higher-level coordination documents;\n   - a `docs/research.md` summarizing incumbent assumptions, alternatives, and what the project will deliberately not do yet.\n4. If the right answer is initially a protocol spec, design document, or interface contract instead of running code, do that first and make the repo reflect it.\n5. Only after the docs and protocols are coherent should implementation begin.\n\n## Bright Builds Requirement Adoption\n\nAdopt the Bright Builds coding and architecture requirements very early, before significant implementation work.\n\nRepository:\n- [bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements](https://github.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements)\n\nPrimary references:\n- [README](https://github.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements)\n- [AI-ADOPTION.md](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/AI-ADOPTION.md)\n\nFollow this exact decision flow:\n\n1. Confirm you are in the root of the downstream repository.\n2. Run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- status\n```\n\n3. If status reports `Repo state: installable`, run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- install --ref main\n```\n\n4. If status reports `Repo state: installed`, run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- update --ref main\n```\n\n5. If status reports `Repo state: blocked`, stop and explain the blocking files instead of forcing replacement automatically.\n6. Only use `install --force` if the user explicitly asks to replace blocked managed files.\n7. After installation or update, mention `coding-and-architecture-requirements.audit.md` as the paper trail.\n\n## Parallel Research Expectations\n\nIf your environment supports subagents, spawn bounded parallel subagents early for:\n\n- protocol and standards research;\n- incumbent and alternative product research;\n- repo/bootstrap and requirements adoption;\n- architecture and system design;\n- implementation planning and test strategy.\n\nIf your environment does not support subagents, still execute those tracks explicitly in sequence and preserve the same separation of concerns.\n\n## Implementation Expectations\n\nAfter the docs and protocols are settled:\n\n1. Choose the smallest credible first release shape: protocol/spec only, backend service, web app, full-stack app, or another justified structure.\n2. Implement the project on top of the documented contracts instead of improvising ad hoc interfaces mid-build.\n3. Prefer root-cause design over shallow feature imitation.\n4. Keep the repo production-shaped early: clear module boundaries, tests, verification commands, and documented assumptions.\n5. If the project should expose APIs, formalize request/response contracts and error behavior before broad feature work.\n\n## Required Deliverables\n\nProduce all of the following unless the user narrows scope:\n\n- repository bootstrap or repository-creation outcome;\n- Bright Builds requirements adoption outcome;\n- protocol and interface research summary;\n- high-level docs and architecture docs;\n- initial implementation plan with milestones or phases;\n- working implementation of the first meaningful slice;\n- tests and verification evidence;\n- explicit residual risks and next steps.\n\n## Done Criteria\n\nDo not declare completion until:\n\n- the docs and protocol decisions are written down;\n- the implementation matches those decisions or explains the deviation;\n- relevant tests, lint, type checks, and build steps have run, or there is a concrete reason they could not run;\n- the user has a clear path to continue shipping the project.",

Treat this as an execution brief for Codex, Claude, OpenCode/OpenClaw, or another capable coding agent.\n\n## Product Context\n\n- Company: United Rentals (URI)\n- Company slug: united-rentals\n- Company description: United Rentals rents construction and industrial equipment and related services to commercial, industrial, utility, municipal, and residential customers.\n- Product: Equipment Rentals\n- Product slug: equipment-rentals\n- Product category: Industrial equipment rental\n- Product homepage: https://www.unitedrentals.com/\n- Product summary: United Rentals rents construction and industrial equipment across general rental and specialty categories, supported by branch logistics, maintenance, safety services, and used-equipment sales.\n- Why it matters: Equipment rental lets contractors and industrial customers avoid owning every machine they need, but it concentrates fleet ownership, pricing power, utilization data, and local availability inside large rental networks.\n\n## Company Thesis\n\n- Rental Network\n  United Rentals is a large-scale equipment rental operator with general rentals, specialty rentals, used equipment sales, service, and digital support layered around a broad rental fleet.\n  The business benefits from fleet density, branch coverage, purchasing scale, utilization data, and customer relationships in construction, industrial maintenance, utilities, government, and infrastructure work.\n- Digital Layer\n  United Rentals complements its physical fleet with UnitedRentals.com, the United Rentals mobile app, Total Control, telematics, billing tools, and system integrations that help customers manage rented equipment across jobsites.\n  This digital control plane strengthens retention because customers can centralize equipment visibility, utilization, pickup requests, analytics, and billing workflows inside United Rentals' platform.\n\n### Moat narrative\n- United Rentals' moat is strongest in physical scale: a large fleet, broad branch network, procurement leverage, logistics density, specialty rental breadth, and the ability to serve large accounts across regions.\n- The moat is reinforced by data and workflow lock-in through Total Control and related digital tools, but it remains exposed to cyclical construction demand, high capital intensity, equipment residual-value risk, and local rental competition.\n\n### Decentralization narrative\n- Equipment rental is only partly decentralizable because heavy equipment requires capital, maintenance capability, safety compliance, transportation, insurance, and local availability. Those constraints favor professional fleet operators.\n- The most credible decentralizing pressure comes from cooperatively owned local fleets, open repair and maintenance tooling, interoperable asset-tracking software, peer-to-peer marketplace coordination, and longer-term open-hardware or microfactory approaches for smaller equipment classes.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- A credible open replacement would start with community, municipal, cooperative, or contractor-owned shared fleets for smaller and mid-sized equipment, using open booking, asset tracking, maintenance logs, and pricing rules.\n- Heavy equipment would remain harder to decentralize, so the practical wedge is local fleet pooling, transparent utilization, repairability, and interoperable software rather than instant replication of United Rentals' full fleet.\n\n## Existing Alternatives Worth Studying First\n\n- Leihbase (open-source): Leihbase is open-source software for lending libraries and local sharing organizations, making it relevant to community-scale equipment pools even though it is not a heavy-equipment rental network.. Homepage: https://leihbase.org/.\n- Open Source Ecology GVCS (open-source): Open Source Ecology's Global Village Construction Set documents open industrial machines and modular production concepts that point toward local fabrication and repair of some equipment categories.. Homepage: https://www.opensourceecology.org/gvcs/.\n\n## Relevant Technology Waves\n\n- Microfactories and automated mini-home production: Small, software-defined manufacturing cells could make localized production less eccentric and more default.\n- Additive manufacturing: 3D plastic and metal printing keep collapsing the minimum viable factory into something much smaller, cheaper, and more local.\n\n## Useful Sources To Read Before Building\n\n- Global Village Construction Set (Open Source Ecology Wiki) - https://wiki.opensourceecology.org/wiki/GVCS\n  Why it matters: Technical and conceptual documentation for the GVCS open-source machine set.\n- GVCS Project Plan (Open Source Ecology Wiki) - https://wiki.opensourceecology.org/wiki/GVCS_Project_Plan\n  Why it matters: Project-plan documentation for GVCS goals, open machinery plans, and prototype assumptions.\n- Leihbase (Leihbase) - https://leihbase.org/\n  Why it matters: Open-source lending-library software relevant to local equipment sharing and cooperative rental coordination.\n- Machines: Global Village Construction Set (Open Source Ecology) - UnitedHealthcare is UnitedHealth Group's health benefits business, serving individuals, employers, Medicare, Medicaid, and other plan markets.\n- Why it matters: It is the company's largest public-facing insurance brand and a major gatekeeper for coverage design, provider access, prior authorization, claims payment, and care navigation.\n\n## Company Thesis\n\n- Business Shape\n technology, data, consulting, and financial services.\n  The company describes its strategic priorities around value-based care, health benefits, health technology, health financial services, and pharmacy services, making it one of the most vertically integrated actors in U.S. health care.\n- Current Financial Snapshot\n  StockAnalysis listed UnitedHealth Group at about $352.8 billion of market capitalization, $449.7 billion of trailing revenue, $12.0 billion of trailing net income, and a 29.32 P/E ratio as of its May 22, 2026 close data.\n  The scale of both insurance membership and Optum services gives UnitedHealth large bargaining, data, distribution, and administrative advantages, but also exposes it to scrutiny over prior authorization, pharmacy benefit management, claims processing, and vertical integration.\n\n### Moat narrative\n- UnitedHealth Group's moat is built on health-plan scale, provider and employer relationships, regulated-plan know-how, Optum's care and data infrastructure, pharmacy benefit management reach, and the operational complexity of claims, utilization management, risk coding, and reimbursement.\n- The moat is durable because payers, providers, employers, pharmacies, and public programs all depend on dense administrative workflows that are costly to replace. Its main weaknesses are regulatory pressure, public backlash around denials and PBM economics, cyber and operational risk, and the possibility that interoperability rules make parts of the data and authorization layer less captive.\n\n### Decentralization narrative\n- UnitedHealth Group is difficult to decentralize because health insurance and care financing depend on regulated risk pools, actuarial capital, licensed networks, medical management, compliance, and negotiated contracts. Those features favor large administrators with deep balance sheets and institutional relationships.\n- The more credible decentralizing pressure is modular rather than total replacement: open-source health financing software, FHIR-based payer APIs, cooperative or public-benefit plan administration, transparent pharmacy purchasing, and federated care records could reduce dependence on a vertically integrated intermediary for narrower workflows.\n\n## Replacement Sketch\n\n- A plausible replacement path would not be a single open insurer. It would be a stack of open health financing administration, interoperable eligibility and claims APIs, transparent benefit rules, and cooperative or public-benefit risk pools.\n- That stack would pressure UnitedHealthcare by making plan administration more portable and auditable, while leaving regulated insurance capital and provider contracting as the hardest pieces to decentralize.\n\n## Existing Alternatives Worth Studying First\n\n- openIMIS (open-source): openIMIS is open-source software for administering health financing and social protection programs, including beneficiaries, providers, claims, contributions, contracts, and accounting.. Homepage: https://openimis.org/. Repo: https://github.com/openimis.\n\n## Relevant Technology Waves\n\n- No technology waves are currently attached. Identify the enabling waves before implementation.\n\n## Useful Sources To Read Before Building\n\n- CMS Interoperability and Prior Authorization Final Rule CMS-0057-F (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services) - https://www.cms.gov/newsroom/fact-sheets/cms-interoperability-prior-authorization-final-rule-cms-0057-f\n  Why it matters: Regulatory source documenting payer FHIR API and prior authorization interoperability requirements relevant to payer lock-in and portability.\n- openIMIS GitHub Organization (openIMIS) - https://github.com/openimis\n  Why it matters: Repository source supporting open-source status and public development of openIMIS components.\n- UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) Stock Price & Overview (StockAnalysis) - https://stockanalysis.com/stocks/unh/\n  Why it matters: Market-data source for market capitalization, P/E ratio, trailing revenue, trailing net income, IPO date, and company profile fields.\n- UnitedHealth Group Investors (UnitedHealth Group) - https://www.unitedhealthgroup.com/investors.html\n  Why it matters: Investor-relations landing page used as a company source for financial reporting and shareholder-facing positioning.\n- What is openIMIS? Start by asking the user whether they already have a repository for this project.\n2. If they do not have a repository yet, ask which platform they want to use.\n3. If the user wants a new repository and a suitable CLI is installed and authenticated, offer to create it on their behalf.\n4. Prefer `gh` for GitHub when available, but support another installed platform CLI if that is what the user is using.\n5. Do not assume repository ownership, visibility, or naming. Restate the target: build a free, open, decentralized, cooperative, or protocol-first alternative that creates real pressure on UnitedHealth Group's UnitedHealthcare business.\n2. Before writing implementation code, research and document the enabling protocols, interfaces, standards, and coordination mechanisms this product should rely on.\n3. Produce the high-level docs first. At minimum create:\n   - a `README.md` with the product thesis and scope;\n   - a `docs/architecture.md` describing the system shape;\n   - a `docs/protocols.md` describing APIs, protocols, data contracts, or higher-level coordination documents;\n   - a `docs/research.md` summarizing incumbent assumptions, alternatives, and what the project will deliberately not do yet.\n4. If the right answer is initially a protocol spec, design document, or interface contract instead of running code, do that first and make the repo reflect it.\n5. Only after the docs and protocols are coherent should implementation begin.\n\n## Bright Builds Requirement Adoption\n\nAdopt the Bright Builds coding and architecture requirements very early, before significant implementation work.\n\nRepository:\n- [bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements](https://github.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements)\n\nPrimary references:\n- [README](https://github.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements)\n- [AI-ADOPTION.md](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/AI-ADOPTION.md)\n\nFollow this exact decision flow:\n\n1. Confirm you are in the root of the downstream repository.\n2. Run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- status\n```\n\n3. If status reports `Repo state: installable`, run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- install --ref main\n```\n\n4. If status reports `Repo state: installed`, run:\n\n```bash\ncurl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bright-builds-llc/coding-and-architecture-requirements/main/scripts/manage-downstream.sh | bash -s -- update --ref main\n```\n\n5. If status reports `Repo state: blocked`, stop and explain the blocking files instead of forcing replacement automatically.\n6. Only use `install --force` if the user explicitly asks to replace blocked managed files.\n7. After installation or update, mention `coding-and-architecture-requirements.audit.md` as the paper trail.\n\n## Parallel Research Expectations\n\nIf your environment supports subagents, spawn bounded parallel subagents early for:\n\n- protocol and standards research;\n- incumbent and alternative product research;\n- repo/bootstrap and requirements adoption;\n- architecture and system design;\n- implementation planning and test strategy.\n\nIf your environment does not support subagents, still execute those tracks explicitly in sequence and preserve the same separation of concerns.\n\n## Implementation Expectations\n\nAfter the docs and protocols are settled:\n\n1. Choose the smallest credible first release shape: protocol/spec only, backend service, web app, full-stack app, or another justified structure.\n2. Implement the project on top of the documented contracts instead of improvising ad hoc interfaces mid-build.\n3. Prefer root-cause design over shallow feature imitation.\n4. Keep the repo production-shaped early: clear module boundaries, tests, verification commands, and documented assumptions.\n5. If the project should expose APIs, formalize request/response contracts and error behavior before broad feature work.\n\n## Required Deliverables\n\nProduce all of the following unless the user narrows scope:\n\n- repository bootstrap or repository-creation outcome;\n- Bright Builds requirements adoption outcome;\n- protocol and interface research summary;\n- high-level docs and architecture docs;\n- initial implementation plan with milestones or phases;\n- working implementation of the first meaningful slice;\n- tests and verification evidence;\n- explicit residual risks and next steps.\n\n## Done Criteria\n\nDo not declare completion until:\n\n- the docs and protocol decisions are written down;\n- the implementation matches those decisions or explains the deviation;\n- relevant tests, lint, type checks, and build steps have run, or there is a concrete reason they could not run;\n- the user has a clear path to continue shipping the project.",
